symbols: Haman hung on a stake in cruciform pose — divine justice reversing the wicked plot; prefiguration of the Cross
Ahasuerus at banquet — the royal authority that reverses injustice when truth is revealed
Esther at banquet — the intercessor who risks her life to save her people
description: A triangular pendentive fresco from the Sistine Chapel ceiling. At center, a powerful muscular male figure (Haman) is bound to a wooden stake or cross-like structure, his body twisted in agony, arms spread. To the left, a seated figure (King Ahasuerus) reclines at a banquet table. To the right, another group includes a reclining figure (Esther) on a bed or couch with attendants. The composition fills the triangular spandrel shape with dramatic foreshortening and monumental anatomy characteristic of Michelangelo's ceiling work.
theological_description: Michelangelo's pendentive depicts the punishment of Haman from the Book of Esther (Esther 7:9-10). Haman, the chief minister of the Persian King Ahasuerus, had plotted to exterminate all Jews in the empire. Queen Esther, herself secretly Jewish, risked her life to reveal the plot at a banquet (Esther 7:3-6). The king ordered Haman to be hanged on the very gallows Haman had prepared for Mordecai (Esther 7:10) — a supreme instance of divine irony and justice. Michelangelo deliberately renders Haman in a cruciform pose, which the Church Fathers (including the Pseudo-Chrysostom and Rabanus Maurus) interpreted as a typological prefiguration of the Crucifixion: the wicked are destroyed by the very instrument that becomes, in Christ, the means of salvation. The three scenes — Esther's banquet plea, Haman's execution, and the king's judgment — compress the narrative into a single visual field, emphasizing the swift reversal that occurs when hidden evil is exposed before righteous authority. The broader theological lesson is that of Proverbs 26:27: 'Whoever digs a pit will fall into it' — God's providential governance ensures that the schemes of the wicked recoil upon their own heads.

scripture_refs.reason: Haman hanged on the gallows he had prepared for Mordecai — the punishment depicted at center
Esther's banquet where she reveals Haman's plot to the king — the flanking scenes
Haman had the gallows built to hang Mordecai — the ironic reversal
Whoever digs a pit will fall into it — the theological principle of divine justice reversing evil
He who digs a hole and scoops it out falls into the pit he has made — the wicked trapped by their own devices

_meta.title: Sistine Chapel Ceiling: The Punishment of Haman
